exemplar.title: Investigating the Recency Effect from the Serial Position Effect in the Multi-Store Memory Model

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Recency Effect from the Serial Position Effect in the Multi-Store Memory Model

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of the aim with explicit relevance to adolescent learning and educational practice
Accurate summary of the multi-store memory model with explicit linkage to the recency effect
Well-operationalized null and research hypotheses specifying IV (40 s distraction) and DV (words recalled from last five positions)
weaknesses: Terminology slip referring to STM/LTM as âsensory registersâ
Some redundancy in phrasing reduces conciseness
Rationale for omitting the 10 s condition and choice of matched-pairs design is not fully justified in the introduction
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction excels in stating a clear, relevant aim and situating it within the multi-store memory model. The hypotheses are fully operationalized, meeting the top band for this strand. Minor terminology inaccuracies and redundant wording prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 reflects high-band performance with room for polish.

strengths: Well-explained matched-pairs independent-groups design with clear rationale against order effects
Description of sampling technique and participant characteristics with inclusion/exclusion criteria
Listing of controlled variables and initial rationales linking them to memory performance
weaknesses: Key procedural steps are relegated to an appendix, reducing main-text clarity
Justification for gender-based matching lacks depth compared to other covariates
Generalizability of convenience sampling to a wider adolescent cohort is only briefly addressed
Rationale for some materials controls (e.g., audio suppression, timing validation) is uneven
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates a solid design explanation and adequate detail on sampling and controls, placing it in the mid-band. However, deferred procedural details and uneven justifications for certain choices limit the depth of explanation, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use and calculation of the Mann-Whitney U test with clear U, p-value, and critical region
Accurate bar graph presentation directly addressing the hypothesis with correct labels
Interpretation links statistical findings to the hypothesis and acknowledges variability and non-significance
weaknesses: Conceptual slip describing the Mann-Whitney U as alternative to a paired t-test rather than independent-groups context
Omission of effect size and confidence intervals limits insight into magnitude and precision
Confusion between mean and median reporting needs clarification
Graph legend accessibility could be improved by labeling bars directly
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis shows strong graphical work and a solid application of inferential statistics, with clear interpretation. A minor conceptual error, absence of effect size, and reporting inconsistencies prevent top marks, placing performance firmly in the 5/6 band.

strengths: Discussion references the multi-store model and original Glanzer & Cunitz findings
Strengths and limitations are identified and explained with clear relevance to validity and reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified to improve design
weaknesses: Theoretical integration is brief and could be deepened with adolescent memory literature
One limitation description is incomplete, reducing clarity
Educational and curricular implications are not revisited in the conclusion
Some discussion remains speculative without empirical support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation includes well-justified strengths, limitations, and linked modifications, but the discussion of theoretical implications and educational applications is cursory. The partial clarity issues and speculative tone keep it in the 3â4 band, so a score of 4 reflects solid but not exemplary performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph compares average recalls for each condition, axes are correctly labelled, numerical values are displayed and the visual directly addresses the hypothesis.  No evident plotting or labelling errors are present, warranting full marks for this strand.

The student reports that the test result is non-significant, explicitly rejects the research hypothesis and accepts the null, and comments on variability (SD) to qualify conclusions.  Interpretation links numbers to the hypothesis, but discussion of effect size or practical meaning is thin, so 5/6 is appropriate.

Both null and research hypotheses are written, the IV (presence/absence of 40-s distraction) and DV (number of correctly recalled words from last five list positions) are fully operationalised, and the predicted direction for the recency section is explicit.  Primacy is also briefly addressed, showing complete precision; therefore the strand meets the top of the 5-6 band.

Convenience sampling is named, demographic characteristics and inclusion/exclusion criteria are given, and brief justification (accessibility, avoidance of IB Psych students) is offered.  The explanation of why this sampling suits the investigation is limited, hence lower half of the top band.

The multi-store memory model is accurately summarised (separate sensory, STM, LTM stores, capacity and duration features) and the serial-position/recency effect is connected to those stores.  The account explicitly states how the present manipulation (distraction during a 40-s delay) is predicted to empty STM and eliminate recency, demonstrating a clear explanatory link.  Minor factual slips (calling the three stores âsensory registersâ) prevent full marks, so a high-band 5 is appropriate.

Findings are compared to Atkinson & Shiffrin and Glanzer & Cunitz, noting lack of support and possible methodological causes.  The discussion is accurate but remains brief and speculative, reaching the 3-4 band (explained but not deeply argued).

The student clearly states the aim â to examine whether a 40-second distraction task diminishes the recency portion of the serial-position curve â and situates this within the broader purpose of testing the short-term vs long-term distinction proposed by the multi-store memory model and its relevance for adolescent learning.  The link to educational practice and to the original Glanzer & Cunitz study shows an explicit explanation of why the investigation matters.  Minor wording redundancies keep the work from exemplary polish, hence 5 rather than 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations for design, sample and procedure are stated and explained with clear relevance to validity and reliability, e.g., matched pairs reduces gender confounds; small convenience sample harms generalisability.  Explanations are coherent though not exhaustive, justifying 5.

Several controlled variables (time of day, environment, word presentation format) and materials are listed with short rationales linking them to memory performance, demonstrating explanation rather than mere listing, but depth is uneven; a mid-band 3 is defensible.

Proposed changes (use repeated measures, increase distraction-task engagement) are explicitly tied to the limitations discussed and are justified in terms of reducing rehearsal or increasing external validity.  The suggestions are feasible and well-linked, warranting placement in the top band though not exceptionally extensive, so 5/6.

The matched-pairs (by gender) independent-groups design is described and the rationale for choosing it over repeated measures (order effects, demand characteristics) is explained, satisfying the requirement for an explanation rather than a surface description.

Means/SDs are calculated; a Mann-Whitney U is chosen because data were non-normal.  Values, critical region and p-value are presented and lead to acceptance of H0.  However, the design is actually independent groups although the text calls the test an alternative to a paired t-test, revealing a conceptual slip.  This minor error fits the 3-4 band with 4 awarded.

annotations.comment: Include a measure of effect size and confidence intervals when reporting U, Z, and p to provide deeper insight into the magnitude and precision of your findings.
The discussion notes contrast with MSM, but integration with Atkinson & Shiffrin theory is brief. Deepen analysis by explaining why distraction may have less impact in adolescents.
The strength of matched pairs is stated, but the sentence ends abruptly (âHowever, a significantâ). Complete this limitation description to maintain clarity.
Sample discussion acknowledges volunteer bias; to enhance relevance, explicitly link how small, convenience-based sampling limits generalizability to multinational samples.
The procedure evaluation notes possible rehearsal in a simple distraction; strengthen this by proposing a concrete interactive distraction (e.g., arithmetic problems), explicitly tying to limitation.
The conclusion restates findings but does not reflect educational implications. Link back to curricula development or adolescent learning to close the loop on relevance.

The introduction effectively situates the aim within the context of adolescent learning, linking the recency effect to educational practice. This explicit explanation of relevance demonstrates strong alignment with the multi-store memory model. Well articulated.
The description of the MSM as three âsensory registersâ is slightly misleading; clarify terminology by distinguishing sensory memory from STM and LTM. Precise naming ensures conceptual accuracy.
The summary of Glanzer & Cunitz (1966) is comprehensive, but the shift from a repeated-measures design to matched pairs in your study is not explicitly justified here. Briefly outline that rationale.
The descriptive section refers to medians then excludes them without clear reasoning. Clarify whether mean or median is primary and justify the choice based on data distribution.
The table of means and SDs is clear, but adding effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) would enhance interpretation of practical significance beyond p-values.
The chosen bar graph directly addresses the hypothesis and uses clear bars for each condition. Excellent graphical technique.
Describing the Mann-Whitney U as an alternative to a paired t-test is conceptually misleading given an independent-groups design; correct this and align your test rationale.
Your null and research hypotheses clearly operationalize IV and DV, with directional prediction for recency. This precision meets best-practice standards for hypothesis writing.
The rationale for omitting the 10-second condition is useful for focus, but consider briefly noting potential trade-offs in generalizability when narrowing conditions.
The DV is described as ânumber of accurate recallsâ but lacks precise operational detail that it refers to the last five list positions. Explicitly state this to avoid ambiguity.
Procedural details are deferred entirely to Appendix 4. To improve clarity for the reader, include a concise outline of key steps here rather than solely referencing the appendix.
The matched-pairs design is well justified against order effects, but clarify why gender matching (rather than other covariates) was most critical for controlling memory variability.
Convenience sampling is described but justification for demographic representativeness is cursory. Expand on why this school-based sample can generalize to a multinational adolescent cohort.

While controlled variables are listed, the rationale for each is uneven. For âExperiment Condition,â explain specifically how individual review prevented bias rather than leaving the bullet generic.
The materials description mentions video standardization but omits detail on audio suppression and timing validation. Include these controls to strengthen replicability.
The graph legend uses identical box symbols; consider adding text labels directly on each bar for clarity and accessibility.
